symbols: raised arms reaching toward light — the universal gesture of worship and surrender to God, the psalmist's 'I will lift up my hands in your name'
white garments — purity, the robes washed white of the redeemed (Revelation 7:14), holiness before God
multiplied shadow-like arms — the intensity and fervency of praise, the Spirit multiplying the offering of worship
descending light from above — the presence of God, the shekinah glory, divine response to human adoration
group of unified figures — the corporate body of worshippers, the assembly of the faithful praising as one
description: A group of figures in flowing white or golden garments stand together with arms raised high toward a brilliant light descending from above. The figures appear to be women, rendered in warm earth tones — ochre, amber, sienna — against a background that transitions from deep brown earth below to luminous golden-white sky above. The arms are multiplied through a motion-blur or layered painting technique, creating the impression of ecstatic, repeated gestures of praise. The faces are tilted upward, expressions of joy and abandon visible.
theological_description: This painting, titled 'Hear My Praise,' directly invokes the biblical tradition of embodied worship through dance and upraised hands. The Psalms are replete with calls to physical praise: 'Lift up your hands in the sanctuary and praise the Lord' (Psalm 134:2); 'Let them praise his name with dancing' (Psalm 149:3). The group of unified figures in white evokes the great multitude of Revelation 7:9 — 'a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation... standing before the throne' — the eschatological worship toward which all earthly praise is oriented. The descending light suggests the reciprocal nature of worship: as the faithful lift their praise upward, God's glory descends to meet them, as when the glory of the Lord filled Solomon's temple (2 Chronicles 7:1). The multiplied arms create a visual rhythm that echoes the repetitive, ecstatic character of praise found in Psalm 136, where 'his love endures forever' is repeated twenty-six times. David's uninhibited dancing before the Ark (2 Samuel 6:14) provides the Old Testament precedent for this kind of whole-bodied, unashamed worship. The title itself echoes the psalmist's plea: 'Hear my cry, O God; listen to my prayer' (Psalm 61:1).

scripture_refs.reason: Lift up your hands in the sanctuary and praise the Lord — the gesture directly depicted in the painting.
Let them praise his name with dancing — embodied worship through movement, the painting's central theme.
The great multitude in white robes standing before the throne crying out in praise, echoed in the white-robed figures.
David danced before the Lord with all his might — the Old Testament precedent for uninhibited worship.
The glory of the Lord filling the temple — the descending light as a sign of divine presence responding to worship.
